topic.title: Expectation-Maximization
topic.question: How does the EM algorithm find local maxima of likelihood for latent-variable models, and why does it always converge?

promptVariant.name: Whiteboard prompt v2 - payoff numbers + sync enforcement

lesson.title: EM Algorithm: Maximizing Likelihood by Filling in Hidden Variables
lesson.learnerLevel: graduate students with probability and optimization background
lesson.payoff: EM can fit a 10-component Gaussian mixture in O(NK) steps per iteration, avoiding the K^N brute-force cost of enumerating every possible assignment of N points to K clusters -- a gap that makes EM the only tractable choice beyond toy data.
staircase: latent-variable likelihood is intractable to maximize directly
Jensen's inequality constructs a computable lower bound on log-likelihood
the E-step computes posterior responsibilities -- the expected complete-data log-likelihood
the M-step maximizes that bound, guaranteed to not decrease the true likelihood
repeating E and M steps monotonically climbs the log-likelihood surface
convergence to a local maximum follows from the bounded monotone sequence theorem
lesson.destination: You will hold the two-step guarantee: Q(theta, theta_old) = E_{Z|X,theta_old}[log p(X,Z|theta)] is the auxiliary function; M-step sets theta_new = argmax_theta Q, and because log p(X|theta_new) >= Q(theta_new, theta_old) >= Q(theta_old, theta_old) = log p(X|theta_old), the observed log-likelihood never decreases, ensuring convergence to a stationary point.

narration.fullText: Suppose you observe data X but each observation came from one of K hidden sources -- the cluster assignments Z were never recorded. The observed log-likelihood, log p of X given theta, requires summing over all possible Z inside the log, and that sum makes direct maximization intractable for even modest K and N. Here is the key insight: Jensen's inequality -- which says that for a concave function f, f of an expectation is at least as large as the expectation of f -- lets us lower-bound the log. If we introduce any distribution q over Z, we can write log p of X given theta as at least the sum over Z of q of Z times log of p of X comma Z given theta divided by q of Z. That lower bound is tight -- it touches the log-likelihood -- when q equals the true posterior p of Z given X comma theta. That touching condition defines the E-step: compute the posterior responsibilities, the probability each data point belongs to each cluster, using the current parameter estimate theta-old. This gives us the auxiliary function Q, defined as the expectation under that posterior of the complete-data log-likelihood log p of X comma Z given theta -- the complete-data log-likelihood treats Z as if it were observed, making the objective separable and closed-form. The M-step then sets theta-new to the argmax -- the argument, meaning the input value, that achieves the maximum -- of Q over theta. Because the lower bound was tight at theta-old, and M-step only increases the bound, we get log p of X given theta-new is at least log p of X given theta-old. The observed log-likelihood never decreases. Since it is also bounded above by zero, the bounded monotone convergence theorem guarantees the sequence converges to a stationary point -- a point where the gradient is zero -- which is a local maximum. Let us trace one full cycle on the board now. We write the objective, draw the likelihood curve with the lower bound touching it, run E then M, and watch the bound lift.

beats.text: Suppose you observe data X but each observation came from one of K hidden sources -- the cluster assignments Z were never recorded. The observed log-likelihood, log p of X given theta, requires summing over all possible Z inside the log, making direct maximization intractable.
beats.whyThisBeat: latent-variable likelihood is intractable to maximize directly

beats.text: Here is the key insight: Jensen's inequality -- which says that for a concave function f, f of an expectation is at least as large as the expectation of f -- lets us lower-bound the log. For any distribution q over Z, log p of X given theta is at least the sum over Z of q of Z times log of p of X comma Z given theta divided by q of Z.
beats.whyThisBeat: Jensen's inequality constructs a computable lower bound on log-likelihood

beats.text: That lower bound is tight -- it touches the log-likelihood -- exactly when q equals the true posterior p of Z given X comma theta. The E-step sets q to this posterior: we compute the responsibilities, the probability each data point belongs to each cluster, under the current theta-old. This defines the auxiliary function Q of theta and theta-old -- the expectation of the complete-data log-likelihood, which treats Z as if it were known, making the objective separable.
beats.whyThisBeat: the E-step computes posterior responsibilities -- the expected complete-data log-likelihood

beats.text: The M-step sets theta-new to the argmax -- the input value that achieves the maximum -- of Q over theta. Because the bound was tight at theta-old before the M-step, and M-step only increases Q, we immediately get that log p of X given theta-new is at least log p of X given theta-old.
beats.whyThisBeat: the M-step maximizes that bound, guaranteed to not decrease the true likelihood

beats.text: Repeating E then M keeps lifting the bound and the true likelihood together. Watch how on the diagram: E-step re-fits the lower bound to touch the curve at the new theta, M-step pushes theta to the peak of that bound, and the cycle continues climbing.
beats.whyThisBeat: repeating E and M steps monotonically climbs the log-likelihood surface

beats.text: Because the observed log-likelihood is bounded above by zero and never decreases, the bounded monotone convergence theorem guarantees convergence to a stationary point -- a point where the gradient is zero -- which is a local maximum. The guarantee is monotone ascent, not global optimality; different initializations can reach different local maxima.
beats.whyThisBeat: convergence to a local maximum follows from the bounded monotone sequence theorem

beats.text: To summarize: write Q of theta and theta-old equals the expectation under p of Z given X theta-old of log p of X comma Z given theta. E-step computes that expectation; M-step maximizes it. The sandwich inequality log p of X given theta-new is at least Q of theta-new theta-old, which is at least Q of theta-old theta-old, which equals log p of X given theta-old, is the complete convergence proof in one line.
beats.whyThisBeat: destination -- precise formula and proof sketch the learner retains

actions.content: Observed log-likelihood: log p(X | theta) = log sum_Z p(X, Z | theta)

Three circles labeled x_1, x_2, x_3 arranged in a column on the right; three circles labeled z_1, z_2, z_3 arranged in a column on the left, each with a directed arrow pointing right to its corresponding x circle; a legend box at bottom reading Z = hidden cluster assignment
K^N terms inside log -- intractable to maximize directly
Jensen: log E[f] >= E[log f]  =>  log p(X|theta) >= sum_Z q(Z) log [p(X,Z|theta) / q(Z)]

A smooth concave curve on axes where x-axis is labeled theta and y-axis is labeled log p(X|theta); a second curve sitting below it, also concave, tangent to the first curve at a point marked theta_old with a vertical dotted line; the gap between curves labeled 'KL divergence >= 0'; the lower curve labeled 'lower bound L(q, theta)'
box the equality condition 'q(Z) = p(Z|X, theta)' in blue to mark when the bound is tight
E-step: compute r_k(x_n) = p(z_n=k | x_n, theta_old)  [responsibilities]
Q(theta, theta_old) = E_{Z|X,theta_old}[log p(X,Z|theta)]
underline 'E_{Z|X,theta_old}' in green to emphasize the expectation is over the posterior at theta_old

A two-column table: left column header 'Complete-data log p(X,Z|theta)', right column header 'Separates into sum over n'; three rows showing sum_n sum_k r_k(x_n) log pi_k plus sum_n sum_k r_k(x_n) log N(x_n; mu_k, Sigma_k); arrow pointing right labeled 'closed-form M-step'
M-step: theta_new = argmax_theta Q(theta, theta_old)
circle 'argmax' in red and annotate: 'argmax = the input value achieving the maximum, not the maximum value itself'
log p(X|theta_new) >= Q(theta_new, theta_old) >= Q(theta_old, theta_old) = log p(X|theta_old)
draw a right-pointing arrow under the full inequality in orange labeled 'likelihood never decreases'

The same theta-axis likelihood curve from likelihood_lowerbound_curve; now a second lower-bound curve tangent at theta_new (to the right of theta_old), with a vertical dotted line at theta_new; an arc arrow from theta_old to theta_new on the x-axis labeled 'M-step'; the lifted tangent point labeled 'E-step re-fits here next'
all
Bounded monotone sequence => converges to stationary point (gradient = 0) = local maximum
box 'stationary point (gradient = 0)' in red to clarify the term means gradient vanishes, not global optimum
all
PROOF IN ONE LINE: log p(X|theta_new) >= Q(theta_new,theta_old) >= Q(theta_old,theta_old) = log p(X|theta_old)
bracket the full three-part inequality in a bold box to mark it as the take-home convergence certificate
